⚙️ Simulation Engineering

FEM Automation Pipeline

A fully automated Abaqus/CAE simulation framework that generates, runs, and post-processes thousands of parametric nanoindentation models — replacing weeks of manual effort with a single Python script execution on an HPC cluster.

● Active — Nov 2024–Present ● Manuscript in Preparation
1000sSimulations automated
100×Faster than manual setup
2D/3DModel fidelity options
<5%Experiment–simulation error

Motivation

Training a machine learning model for inverse material property prediction requires a large, high-quality simulation dataset — ideally thousands of FEM runs spanning the full parameter space of interest (material properties, indenter geometry, film thickness, friction). Manual model setup in Abaqus/CAE GUI for each parameter combination is completely infeasible at this scale.

The automation pipeline solves this by scripting every stage of the Abaqus workflow in Python: geometry creation, mesh generation, material assignment, boundary conditions, job submission, output extraction, and post-processing — all parameterized and batch-executable on a Linux HPC cluster.

Engineering goal

Generate a simulation dataset large enough and diverse enough to train a neural network that can accurately predict material properties (E, H, σ_y) from any experimental nanoindentation load–displacement curve.

Pipeline architecture

01

Parameter space definition

A configuration file defines the sweep: yield stress σ_y, elastic modulus E, hardening exponent n, indenter radius R, friction coefficient μ, and indentation depth. Latin Hypercube Sampling ensures efficient coverage of the high-dimensional input space.

02

Automated model build

A Python script uses the Abaqus scripting interface (abaqus.py) to programmatically create the full model: cylindrical specimen geometry (45 μm × 25 μm), spherical or conical indenter, mesh with contact refinement at the indentation zone, elastic-plastic material via power-law hardening, and Coulomb friction contact.

03

HPC batch submission

Jobs are submitted to the UTK HPC cluster via SLURM batch scripts. Each simulation runs Abaqus/Standard with parallel CPU allocation. The pipeline monitors job completion and handles failures with automatic retry logic.

04

Automated post-processing

Upon completion, an ODB extraction script parses the output database to retrieve force–displacement curves, Mises stress fields, and contact area evolution. Data is normalized and written to structured CSV/JSON files ready for ML ingestion.

05

Quality control

Automated checks flag simulations with convergence issues, excessive mesh distortion, or anomalous load–displacement behavior. Flagged runs are automatically re-queued with adjusted solver settings.

Simulation overview

Comprehensive Abaqus FEA indentation simulation: Model A (spherical indenter) and Model B (actual conical tip), Mises stress time-step evolution, and final-state contact mesh refinement with substrate and surface stress distributions.

Key results

<5%
Force–displacement error vs. experiment (Cu wire, Mo, W)
Coarse
Mesh validated for bulk prediction — 10× faster than fine mesh
Friction
μ = 0.1–0.3 has minimal effect on bulk response; critical for thin films
2D vs 3D
Axisymmetric 2D captures bulk response; 3D needed for grain-scale effects

Visual explainer

Research workflow

The full pipeline: thousands of parametric Abaqus simulations generate force–displacement data, which feeds an automated ML training workflow to perform inverse prediction of elasticity, hardness, and plasticity — saving hundreds of engineering hours.

End-to-end pipeline: Abaqus FEM simulation → automated data processing → ML training → inverse material property prediction.
